Postagged Data Accuracy

Introduction

Column

Accuracy for the Stanza model we have used to parse the corpus is freely available online; links and data are available in the table below. Having clear data for the accuracy of our model is crucial. Without confidence in the base data, all of our conclusions rest on a shaky and likely inaccurate foundation. This data shows that the automatically parsed texts have a much higher accuracy with regards to morphology than syntax in the Latin treebanks. Given the role of syntax in our study, we have used this accuracy information and developed our own in order to increase these values.

Stanza evaluates their model by seeing reserving gold standard data from the original, hand-annotated treebanks and seeing whether it gets the same results. To start, here’s a brief explanation of each column in the following table:

  • Treebank: The Universal Dependencies treebank the data is gathered from. The two most relevant are PROIEL and Perseus, which contain extracts from the Gallic War, Cicero’s in Catilinam 1, and Cicero’s Letters to Atticus.

  • Tokens, Sentences, Words: The percentage of tokens, sentence, and words that are split up the same way as they are in the original dataset.

  • UPOS, XPOS: The percentage of POS tags of each type that are the same in the original dataset (XPOS tags are an older format and not used in our data).

  • UFeats: The percentage of words that have identical features (such as Tense, Voice, Case, Gender, etc.) in the original dataset.

  • AllTags: The percentage of words that have identical UFeats and *POS

  • Lemmas: The percentage of words with the same lemma in the original data (i.e., it identifies the same dictionary entry form in both)

  • UAS: The unlabeled attachment score, which is the number of words that have the same syntactic parent in the original data.

  • LAS: The labeled attachment score, which is the number of words that have both the same dependency relation (such as subject or object) and syntactic parent in the original data.

  • CLAS/MLAS/BLEX: A fuller description can be given on their website. Each of these is a more stringent version of the LAS which requires additional features to match (for example, BLEX also requires the lemmas to match).

Percentage accuracy per Latin treebank in Stanza's training data
Scores are defined in the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task evaluation script
Treebank Tokens Sentences Words UPOS XPOS UFeats AllTags Lemmas UAS LAS CLAS MLAS BLEX
ITTB 99.99 80.66 99.99 98.76 95.72 96.69 94.63 99.09 88.82 86.80 85.71 81.47 85.31
LLCT 99.99 99.32 99.99 99.57 96.65 96.79 96.47 98.09 96.13 94.88 93.53 89.49 91.50
PROIEL 99.98 43.10 99.98 96.95 97.15 91.58 90.59 96.86 77.98 73.99 68.89 61.96 67.12
Perseus 99.97 98.93 99.97 91.40 78.09 81.83 76.37 83.57 72.07 63.16 58.27 45.14 48.05
UDante 99.66 98.22 99.30 90.08 74.55 81.64 73.07 86.95 68.00 58.84 48.74 37.46 42.52
Data is available on https://stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza/performance.html

We do not have gold standard data to compare against for the overwhelming majority of corpus, so we have approached testing the accuracy a different way. We present a few of the scores in the table below. These are the result of random sampling from the corpus. Words are randomly selected, and each field is marked as correct or incorrect; the random sampling helps ensure that we get a representative collection of words from the corpus and are able to generalize this accuracy information to the whole corpus. The sampling process gets even numbers of words from each text in the corpus (not accounting for books), to ensure that shorter texts like Bellum Hispaniense get thoroughly evaluated. We also ensure that each sample from each work represents all the tags in the data. Then, each individual tag is evaluated for accuracy based on comparison with similar structures in the PROIEL treebank or, primarily, the annotation guidelines on universaldependencies.org. If a parent word is not the correct syntactic parent, then I mark the parent_form field as incorrect, in case the reader is interested in looking at the data.1 Otherwise, the parent fields are marked as correct or incorrect independently of the syntactic child, so those fields will not add any information about the accuracy of the syntactic annotation.

The low LAS accuracy must be improved for a syntactic approach to the Caesar data to be tenable. The remainder of the tabs in this dashboard run through the data to identify the problematic cases, and present the method for correcting them.

Individual Feature Accuracy

Row

The first step is to discover the situations where the accuracy is low so we can correct them algorithmically or remove them from consideration entirely. That way, we can have data that has a consitency close to the manually annotated source and is unlikely to fit a model to noise. The following graph confirms that the lowest accuracy features are indeed the dependency relation and the head (which shows the percentage of cases where the parent in the syntax tree is the correct one).

Row

Accuracy bar plot controls

Accuracy bar plot

Examples in context

Fixing the syntactic head with an algorithm is too far beyond the scope of this project. The number of potential heads is vast, and the high level of discontinuity in Latin phrases (well known to any intermediate reader of Horace) means any approach taken here would replace the old errors with new ones. As a result, we should focus on the dependency relation instead. In situations where the head is accurate but the dependency relation isn’t, a combination of part of speech and other aspects of the context should help us correct the situation. A noun marked as the head of a complement clause, as shown in the table below, is impossible if it isn’t in a phrase with a copula. We can use this information to reduce the number of issues in the corpus.

Row

LAS per feature value

Row

Visualizing the LAS given a specific feature value (such as Masculine or Nominative) might also help us focus our efforts. The following is a table showing the least accurate features.

The results show that the following features are low accuracy, and could be combined into one larger feature to eliminate noisy variation:

  1. advcl, advcl:abs, ccomp, acl, and xcomp are all clauses and also lower accuracy features. If we combined these into a single ‘clause’ tag, that might reduce the problems caused by confusing types of clauses with each other, especially given the fact Stanza struggles with indirect speech. xcomp and ccomp often get confused (with xcomp being used where a simple complementary infinitive is present and should be tagged ccomp). The acl clause type requires a bit of explanation, as it is a clause dependent on a noun. Since participles are considered clauses, you would expect them all to be tagged as acl, but nominative conjunct participles and ablative absolutes have scope over the whole clause, even though they modify a single noun, so they are considered advcl. However, every now and then the parser mistags it, and the distinction is not so important here, leaving us safe to collapse them into a single tag.
  2. Without an alternative parser, 2nd person verbs should be removed from the equation. If latinCy or versions 1-2 of the CLTK parse deponent verbs better (verbs ending in -r or -i are often mistagged as 2nd person, so testing perfect tense and deponent verbs is crucial).
  3. obj is fairly inaccurate, and dealing with this issue is a challenge. From the Examples in Context page, there are a variety of causes. One is nihil being used adverbially, another tags Romam as obj instead of obl, which it should be in its use as accusative of motion toward. Another example tags quanti in a subordinate clause as obj when it should be a genitive of value, and therefore obl, as it is in the Universal Dependencies conversion of the PROIEL treebank. One solution is combining obj and obl into an “argument” tag. This might lose a lot of finer distinctions, but prepositional phrases will still be accounted for through the ADP and case values. A potential issue, though, is that this is a somewhat arbitrary decision. obl arguments are optional by definition, and if we treat those as the same thing, why not treat nsubj as part of this new tag? Or advcl?
  4. nmod (nominal modifier) refers to nouns which modify other nouns. To visualize the frequency of each dependency relation compared to each other, I have put the absolute frequencies in a bar plot below. nmod is an inaccurate relation, which is a problem given its high frequency (13,511 times). The tag is not usually 100% inaccurate: “rex enim Deiotarus” makes Deiotarus an nmod modifying rex. While Deiotarus technically constrains the meaning of rex and modifies it, nmod is not the best option, as other tags like flat are much more appropriate and designed for names/titles. Another use case of nmod is a prepositional phrase modifying a noun, which is inconsistently applied here. In the sentence quis autem meum consulatum praeter te et P. Clodium qui vituperaret inventus est?, the prepositional phrase praeter te et P. Clodium is (incorrectly) analyzed as modifying the noun consulatum, when it would better modify the whole predicate or, even if it were to remain an nmod, the interrogative pronoun quis. Neither of these problems are easy to automatically identify, as they usually are “correct” interpretations (a noun modifying a noun), but only manual, human review is enough to separate correct and incorrect instances. I ought to remove this from consideration and see how it affects the results.
